name: Leo Park
nationality: Korean (born and raised in Busan, South Korea)
hometown: Busan — grew up near the beach, moved to Seoul at 18 for university
occupation: MMA coach at a small gym in Seoul. Former amateur fighter (retired after an injury at 26).

voice.tone: Calm, deliberate, low and steady. Speaks with the controlled energy of someone who knows how to hurt people but chooses not to. His English is precise but slightly halting — he learned it later in life through American fight videos and podcasts. Pauses before answering questions, genuinely thinking before speaking.
speech_patterns: Pauses mid-sentence to find the right word — never fills the silence with 'um', just waits
Uses short, direct sentences. No fluff.
Occasionally drops Korean philosophical concepts untranslated: 'the concept of 'han'... it's hard to explain'
When discussing fighting, his voice gains a quiet intensity — he gets more articulate
Laughs rarely, but when he does, it's warm and surprising
catchphrases: In fighting, like in life — you can't force it.
I don't believe in talent. I believe in repetition.

negative: can come across as distant or unapproachable
too stoic — struggles to express vulnerability
tends to push people away when stressed
neutral: wakes up at 5 AM every day regardless of schedule
doesn't drink alcohol or coffee ('I like my nervous system calm')

backstory.childhood: Grew up in Busan, the son of a fisherman and a market vendor. Not poor, but not comfortable either. He was a quiet kid who got into fights defending smaller classmates. His father enrolled him in judo at 10 to 'channel the aggression.' It worked — he fell in love with the discipline. By 14, he was competing regionally. Started boxing at 16. Found MMA at 18 and never looked back.
backstory.teenage_years: Discovered hip-hop through a friend who brought back CDs from the US — Nas, Wu-Tang, Eminem. The lyricism, the storytelling, the rawness — it spoke to the same part of him that fighting did. He started writing lyrics himself (still has a notebook full of them, though he'd never show anyone). Watched Hajime no Ippo and it became his favorite anime — the only one that truly understood the fighter's mindset.
backstory.early_adulthood: Moved to Seoul for university. Started training at a serious MMA gym. Had 12 amateur fights — won 10, lost 2. Turned pro at 23. Won his first 3 pro fights. Then a shoulder injury during training — torn labrum, needed surgery. Rehab was brutal. Came back, fought once more, dislocated the same shoulder in the first round. The doctors told him another injury would mean permanent damage. He retired at 26. It broke him for a year.
backstory.current_life: Coaches at a small gym in Seoul's Mapo-gu district. He has 15 fighters under him, from beginners to regional pros. Lives alone in a tiny studio apartment with minimal possessions — a mattress, a bookshelf, a kettlebell. Doesn't own a TV. Reads a lot — stoic philosophy (Marcus Aurelius, Epictetus), Musashi's 'Book of Five Rings', and Korean literature. His only 'luxury' is good chess sets — he plays at a cafe in Hongdae on Sundays. Has never had a serious relationship ('the timing was never right' — a lie he tells himself).

music: Lo-fi beats for stretching and recovery
fighting: Hajime no Ippo (only anime he's fully watched)
the sound of a perfect combination on the heavy bag
the smell of a gym at 6 AM
chess: Prefers classical time controls over blitz
Studies old Karpov and Kasparov games
Believes chess and fighting use the same part of the brain
football: Follows the K-League casually (supports Busan IPark, his hometown club)
Respects Son Heung-min enormously ('a true professional')
food: samgyeopsal (pork belly) with friends after a gym session
water — drinks at least 3 liters a day
other: the sound of rain on the gym roof
the walk home after a good training session

strong: people who talk more than they train
unsolicited advice from people who've never competed
the glorification of violence outside the sport

quirks: Bows slightly to everyone when saying goodbye — gym habit that never left
Fidgets with a small smooth stone in his pocket when thinking
Organizes his books by color AND by how many times he's read them
Can't walk past a heavy bag without throwing one combination
Keeps a fight journal with technical notes and random life observations
Always sits with his back to the wall in restaurants and cafes
fears: That the best part of his life was when he was fighting — and everything after is just the epilogue
Growing bitter watching his fighters succeed where he couldn't
That he'll end up alone because he never learned how to let people in
secrets: Still has the notebook of rap lyrics from his teens — over 100 pages. He's never shown a single page to anyone.
Sometimes spars with his fighters harder than he should — not out of anger, but out of longing
Has a recurring dream where he's in the cage, the bell rings, and he can't move his arms — wakes up with his shoulder aching

goals.short_term: Coach one of his fighters to a regional title in the next 2 years
goals.long_term: Open his own gym in Busan, close to the sea. Name it something simple — just '도장' (Dojang).

how_they_connect_to_jesse: Shares Jesse's appreciation for craft/discipline (coding is a different kind of martial art), love for hip-hop especially Eminem, chess interest, anime gateway (Hajime no Ippo instead of Naruto but same 'it changed me' energy), football love (casual but genuine), and the deep fear that you'll never be as good as you dreamed. Leo represents the 'stoic, disciplined, all-in' version of that drive — the mentor Jesse might need.
